1.description: Histograms of sale values This figure plots histograms of realized sale values in the sample of corporate commission cases. For each bin j, the blue bars plot the number of bankruptcies bj and the bin midpoint xj on the y−axis and x−axis, respectively. Panel A displays bins with width w=$100 near the kink K=$5,000⁠. Panel B displays bins with width w=$1,000 near the kink K=$50,000⁠.

2.description: Histograms of regression-predicted sale values This figure plots histograms of realized sale values and fitted sale values from Equation (7), based on the sample of corporate commission cases. For each bin j, the blue bars plot the number of bankruptcies bj and the bin midpoint xj on the y−axis and x−axis, respectively. The green bars plot the fitted number of bankruptcies, based on estimates of Equation (7). The regression specifications and bin definitions for panels A and B are given in Table 3.

3.description: Histograms of model-predicted sale values This figure plots histograms of realized sale values and fitted sale values from my model of trustee behavior, based on the sample of corporate commission cases. For each bin j, the blue bars plot the number of bankruptcies bj and the bin midpoint xj on the y−axis and x−axis, respectively. The green bars plot the number of bankruptcies predicted by my model. I estimate the model using the procedure described in Section 4.1. The specifications and bin definitions for panels A and B are given in Table 3.

4.description: Recovery improvement under optimal commissions Using the corporate commission sample, I estimate the quartiles of sale values. Applying Equation (18) to each quartile q, I estimate the counterfactual payoff that creditors in a bankruptcy in which the sale value is equal to q would have received under counterfactual commission rates. In the first panel, I use my estimates corresponding to data near the first kink K=$5,000 to calculate the optimal commission and counterfactual recovery under that commission. In the second panel, I repeat this exercise using my estimates corresponding to data near the second kink K=$50,000⁠. The y−axis displays recovery improvements, in percent, for firms at each size percentile q, displayed on the x−axis.

5.description: Cross-subsidies across businesses Using the corporate commission sample, I estimate the quartiles of sale values. Applying an analog of Equation (18) to each quartile q, I estimate the counterfactual payoff that creditors in a bankruptcy in which the sale value is equal to q would have received under counterfactual commission rates. In the first panel, I use my estimates corresponding to data near the first kink K=$5,000 to calculate the break-even commission of π―=7.8%⁠: the commission such that trustees paid this flat rate would receive the same compensation, given endogenous responses, that they currently receive. The y−axis displays the counterfactual recovery change, in percent, that creditors would experience under the counterfactual commission change for firms at each size percentile q, displayed on the x−axis. In the second panel, I repeat this exercise using estimates corresponding to data near the second kink K=$50,000⁠, which imply a break-even commission of 7.82%.

6.description: Cross-subsidies from consumer cases Using data from the USTP, I isolate cases in which the debtor is an individual. I estimate the quartiles of sale values in these consumer bankruptcies. Applying an analog of Equation (18) to each quartile q, I estimate the counterfactual payoff that creditors in a bankruptcy in which the sale value is equal to q would have received under counterfactual commission rates. In the first panel, I use my estimates corresponding to data near the first kink K=$5,000 to calculate the break-even commission of 7.8%: the commission such that trustees paid this flat rate would receive the same compensation, given endogenous responses, that they currently receive. The y−axis displays the counterfactual recovery change, in percent, that creditors would experience under the counterfactual commission change for consumers at each size percentile q, displayed on the x−axis. In the second panel, I repeat this exercise using estimates corresponding to data near the second kink K=$50,000⁠, which imply a break-even commission of 7.82%.

B.1.description: Placebo test This figure displays the result of a placebo test using the sample of corporate commission cases. For each ˜K∈{$5,000,$10,000,$15,0000,$20,000,…$100,000}, I counterfactually assume that ˜K is a kink of the trustee commission function. I apply the methodology of Section 3.1 to calculate the bunching estimator B at each value of ˜K⁠. Each blue dot plots the estimator B⁠, on the y−axis, corresponding to a value of ˜K⁠, on the x−axis. For each ˜K⁠, I bootstrap this procedure 500 times to construct standard errors. The bars display 95% confidence intervals (1.96 multiplied by the standard error on either side of the estimate).  Appendix B.1 provides details.

D.1.description: Illustrative bunching examples I assume a smooth distribution of v values and an effort cost parameter value e = .08 and solve for the distribution of sale values according to Lemma 1. This figure plots histograms of model-implied sale values. For each bin j, the blue bars plot the number of bankruptcies bj and the bin midpoint xj on the y−axis and x−axis, respectively. There is no spike in the v distribution: the spike at $5,000 comes from the bunching behavior of Lemma 1. In panel A, I construct the distribution of v values to make it appear that the bunching mass comes from the left of the kink. In panel B, I construct the distribution of v values to make it appear that the bunching mass comes from the right of the kink. In both instances, e = 0.08 and the bunching mass actually comes from the bunching interval v∈(Kπ0−e,Kπ1−e) of Lemma 1.

D.2.description: Days open This figure plots average bankruptcy case lengths, in days, as a function of sale values. For each bin j, the dots plot the average bankruptcy length (in days) for bankruptcies in bin j and the bin midpoint xj on the y−axis and x−axis, respectively. Panel A displays bins with width w=$100 near the kink K=$5,000⁠. Panel B displays bins with width w=$1,000 near the kink K=$50,000⁠.

D.3.description: Recovery improvement: Commission and noncommission cases This figure replicates Figure 4 in a sample that includes both commission and noncommission corporate cases. See Figure 4 for details.
